title: Research Philosophy
simple_html:
Research philosophy is the set of beliefs you hold about what reality is and what counts as valid knowledge. It is the outermost layer of the research onion because every later choice flows from it. Even researchers who never think about philosophy still have one — making it explicit just makes your study more honest and consistent.

Two key questions

  • Ontology — What is the nature of reality? Is there one objective world out there (realism), or is reality shaped by how people interpret it (relativism)?
  • Epistemology — How do we know what we know? Through measurable facts, or through meanings and interpretation?

The main philosophies

  • Positivism — reality is objective and measurable; the researcher stays detached and tests hypotheses with numbers.
  • Interpretivism — reality is socially constructed; the researcher gets close to people to understand meanings, usually with words.
  • Realism / critical realism — a real world exists, but our knowledge of it is filtered and imperfect.
  • Pragmatism — pick whatever works to answer the question; mixes methods freely.

Key takeaways

  • Philosophy = beliefs about reality (ontology) and knowledge (epistemology).
  • Positivism leans quantitative; interpretivism leans qualitative.
  • Pragmatism is question-led and supports mixed methods.
  • Your philosophy must stay consistent with your methods.
  • There is no single "correct" philosophy — only one that fits your question.
intermediate_html:
Research philosophy refers to the system of beliefs and assumptions about the development of knowledge that underpins a study. It is the foundation layer of the research onion because it shapes every subsequent decision — approach, strategy, methods, and analysis. Two questions define a philosophical position, and a third dimension (axiology) addresses values.

The three building blocks

  • Ontology — assumptions about the nature of reality. Objectivism holds that social entities exist independently of our perception; subjectivism/constructionism holds that they are produced through social interaction and are always being revised.
  • Epistemology — assumptions about what constitutes acceptable knowledge: observable, measurable facts versus subjective meanings and narratives.
  • Axiology — the role of the researcher's values: value-free and detached, or value-laden and reflexive.

The major paradigms

PhilosophyOntologyEpistemologyTypical methods
PositivismOne real, external worldObservable facts, law-like generalisationsQuantitative, deductive, large samples
Critical realismReal but stratified worldKnowledge is socially situated, fallibleMixed; explains underlying mechanisms
InterpretivismMultiple constructed realitiesMeanings, lived experienceQualitative, inductive, small samples
PostmodernismReality shaped by power/languageKnowledge is contested, partialCritical, deconstructive
PragmatismReality is practical, pluralWhatever usefully answers the questionMixed methods, abductive

Worked example

Two researchers study workplace stress. Researcher A is a positivist: she defines stress with a validated scale, surveys 500 employees, and uses regression to test whether workload predicts stress scores. Researcher B is an interpretivist: he wants to understand how employees experience and talk about stress, so he conducts 15 in-depth interviews and analyses themes. Neither is wrong; they answer different questions with internally consistent designs. A pragmatist (Researcher C) might do both — survey to map prevalence, interviews to explain the mechanisms.

How to apply it

  1. Read your research question's verbs. "Measure, test, predict, relate" point to positivism; "explore, understand, interpret, experience" point to interpretivism.
  2. State your ontology and epistemology explicitly in your methodology chapter — examiners expect this.
  3. Check downstream consistency: your approach (Lesson 3), strategy, and analysis must all match the stance you declared.
  4. Be reflexive about axiology: acknowledge how your own values and role might shape findings, especially in qualitative work.

The common exam trap is to declare a philosophy for show and then contradict it in the methods — e.g., claiming interpretivism but reporting only closed-ended Likert frequencies. Philosophy is the promise; the rest of the onion must keep it. This layer feeds directly into Approaches & Research Strategies (Lesson 3) and is tested in practice through the design choices of Lessons 4–8.
full_html:
Research philosophy is the system of beliefs and assumptions about the nature and development of knowledge that grounds a study (Saunders, Lewis & Thornhill, 2019). It is the outermost layer of the research onion and the most consequential, because it silently constrains every choice that follows. The point of studying it is not to convert students to a creed but to make tacit assumptions explicit, so that a study's design is coherent and its claims appropriately scoped. As Crotty (1998) argues, methods derive from methodology, methodology from a theoretical perspective, and that perspective from an epistemology; philosophy is therefore the root of the justificatory chain.

The dimensions of a philosophical position

A research philosophy is specified along three axes. Ontology concerns the nature of reality: does an objective social world exist independently of our knowing it (objectivism/realism), or is reality continually constructed through social interaction (constructionism/subjectivism)? Epistemology concerns what we can know and how: positivists privilege observable, quantifiable facts and seek law-like generalisations, whereas interpretivists privilege subjective meanings, narratives and lived experience (Bryman & Bell, 2015). Axiology concerns the place of values: positivism aspires to a value-free, detached researcher, while interpretivism treats research as value-laden and demands reflexivity about the researcher's influence (Heron & Reason, 1997).

The five paradigms in management research

Positivism (after Comte and the logical positivists) assumes a single external reality knowable through the senses and measurement; it favours deduction, hypothesis testing, large probability samples, and statistical analysis. Its great strength is replicability and generalisation; its great weakness is reducing rich social phenomena to thin variables. Critical realism (Bhaskar, 1975) accepts a real world but argues it is stratified into the empirical (what we observe), the actual (events whether observed or not), and the real (the underlying causal mechanisms). The critical realist's goal is to explain the mechanisms that generate observable patterns, which makes the stance especially congenial to mixed methods and to explaining, not merely describing. Interpretivism (drawing on Weber's Verstehen, phenomenology, and symbolic interactionism) holds that the social world differs fundamentally from the natural world because people attach meaning to their actions; understanding therefore requires getting close to participants through qualitative, inductive inquiry. Postmodernism emphasises the role of language and power in constituting what passes for knowledge, foregrounding marginalised voices and deconstructing dominant accounts. Pragmatism (Dewey, Peirce, James) rejects forced choices between positivism and interpretivism: the research question dictates the method, truth is what works in practice, and mixed methods are natural (Tashakkori & Teddlie, 1998).

Worked illustration

Consider the construct "leadership effectiveness." A positivist operationalises it as a score on a multi-item scale, hypothesises that transformational leadership predicts team performance, and tests this on 300 teams using structural equation modelling. A critical realist accepts the survey evidence but probes why the relationship holds — what mechanisms (trust, psychological safety) generate it — perhaps combining the survey with interviews. An interpretivist brackets the question of whether leadership "causes" performance and instead asks how followers experience and co-construct leadership in their daily talk, using ethnography. A pragmatist sequences these: a survey to establish prevalence and a qualitative phase to explain it. The same topic, four defensible philosophies, four very different studies — each internally coherent.

Nuances and debates

The so-called paradigm wars of the 1980s–90s pitted positivists against interpretivists over the very possibility of combining them, given the incompatibility thesis (Guba & Lincoln, 1994) that paradigms rest on irreconcilable assumptions. Pragmatists countered that paradigms are tools, not prisons, and that the dichotomy is overdrawn (Tashakkori & Teddlie, 1998); most contemporary methods texts now treat philosophy as a continuum rather than a set of warring camps. A second debate concerns reflexivity: interpretivists insist the researcher is part of what is studied and must account for their positionality, whereas positivists treat researcher effects as bias to be eliminated. A third nuance is that philosophy is partly a matter of the researcher's own assumptions, which may be neither fully conscious nor fully consistent; honest scholarship involves examining and declaring them.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Philosophy as decoration. Declaring "this study adopts an interpretivist philosophy" and then reporting only Likert-scale frequencies. The stated philosophy must govern the actual methods.
  • Conflating ontology with epistemology. They are distinct questions (what exists vs. how we know); a clean position addresses both.
  • Equating positivism with "quantitative" and interpretivism with "qualitative." The correlation is strong but not absolute — one can count qualitatively or interpret quantitatively; the link runs through approach, not by definition.
  • Assuming one philosophy is superior. Fitness for the research question, not prestige, is the criterion.
  • Ignoring axiology in qualitative work, leaving researcher influence unexamined.

Cross-links

Philosophy is the parent of the next onion layer, Approaches & Research Strategies (Lesson 3), where deductive/inductive/abductive reasoning and concrete strategies are matched to the paradigm. It also shapes how validity and trustworthiness are conceived — positivists speak of reliability and validity, interpretivists of trustworthiness criteria (Lesson 7). Establishing your philosophy early, as the research onion (Lesson 1) demands, is what gives the rest of your dissertation its internal logic.

concepts.label: Nature of business research / research onion
